title: Listening - Part 4: Radio Football Commentary
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Chloe, who commentates on football matches for radio. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This evening my guest is Chloe Wright, who commentates on local football matches for radio. Chloe, how did you begin talking about sport on air? || Chloe: I played football at school but had no plans for broadcasting. At university I helped run the student radio station and usually chose music. One Saturday the sports presenter was ill just before a match. [24]I agreed to describe the game at short notice and was amazed by how much I enjoyed helping listeners imagine the action[/24]. I began attending matches with a recorder to improve. || Presenter: Is commentating mostly about speaking quickly? || Chloe: Speed helps when there is sudden action, but words are only useful when they are accurate. Listeners cannot see the pitch, so the commentator must provide a clear picture. [25]Before every match I learn the players’ names and shirt numbers thoroughly so I can identify them instantly[/25]. Guessing the wrong player confuses everyone. || Presenter: What was difficult in your first broadcasts? || Chloe: I felt I had to fill every second with speech. If nothing exciting happened, I repeated myself or gave unimportant details. An experienced colleague made me listen to my recordings and said, [26]‘A brief silence is better than talking when you have nothing useful to add.’[/26] That was hard advice to follow initially, but it improved my commentary. || Presenter: Have you ever made an embarrassing mistake live on air? || Chloe: During one cup match I shouted that the home team had scored, only to realise the ball had hit the outside of the net. I wanted to disappear. However, [27]I corrected myself immediately and listeners later said they appreciated that I did not pretend the mistake had not happened[/27]. Live broadcasting teaches you to recover quickly. || Presenter: Has social media affected your job? || Chloe: It provides immediate reactions, sometimes before the match is over. Criticism can be harsh, but useful messages arrive too. [28]Supporters who cannot attend games often tell me which details help them follow what is happening, and I use that feedback[/28]. Comments from actual listeners can improve the service more than guessing what they need. || Presenter: Does being a woman in football broadcasting matter? || Chloe: Occasionally somebody assumes I know less about the game, which is irritating. But the majority judge the quality of my work. [29]I have found that being thoroughly prepared is the most effective response to anyone who doubts my ability[/29]. Once a broadcast begins, knowledge and clarity are what count. || Presenter: What is your ambition now? || Chloe: People expect me to say national television, but I enjoy radio enormously. [30]My aim is to cover higher-level matches while keeping the challenge of creating the whole scene through words alone[/30]. For me, that is what makes commentary exciting.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Chloe first become interested in sports commentary?

options.A: She volunteered unexpectedly for a match broadcast.
options.B: She recorded matches as part of her football training.
options.C: A radio station invited her after hearing her play.

questions.explanation: She filled in for an absent presenter and enjoyed describing the match.

questions.question: Why does Chloe study shirt numbers before a match?

options.A: To judge how the teams are likely to play.
options.B: To recognise players quickly while commentating.
options.C: To provide listeners with statistics about players.

questions.explanation: She prepares names and numbers so she can immediately identify players.

questions.question: What advice helped Chloe improve her early broadcasts?

options.A: Use silence rather than speaking without useful information.
options.B: Speak more quickly when a match is quiet.
options.C: Repeat important information for new listeners.

questions.explanation: Her colleague advised that a short silence was preferable to meaningless speech.

questions.question: What happened after Chloe made an error in a cup match?

options.A: She was asked to leave the broadcast early.
options.C: The home team complained about her report.

questions.explanation: She corrected the false goal call immediately and listeners appreciated her honesty.

questions.question: How does social media help Chloe?

options.A: It lets her avoid attending less important matches.
options.B: It provides football news before a match starts.
options.C: It tells her what information listeners find useful.

questions.explanation: Supporters give feedback about the details that help them follow matches.

questions.question: How does Chloe respond to people who doubt her knowledge?

options.A: She prepares carefully for her broadcasts.
options.B: She discusses the issue during commentary.
options.C: She avoids reading their comments online.

questions.explanation: She says thorough preparation is the most effective response.

questions.question: What does Chloe hope to do in the future?

options.A: Move from radio to national television.
options.B: Commentate on more important games for radio.

questions.explanation: Her goal is higher-level matches while retaining radio's descriptive challenge.
